channel.title: Luke Myer MP for Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland
channel.description: From and for Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland

image.title: Luke Myer MP for Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland

item.title: Families to save up to £1,000 as children’s reforms become law
item.dc:creator: luke
item.description: Thousands of children across Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland constituency are set to benefit from sweeping new support for families after Labour’s flagship Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Act officially became law this week (Wednesday 29 April 2026). The wide-ranging reforms — backed by local MP Luke Myer and opposed by both Conservatives and Reform — […]
item.content:encoded:
Thousands of children across Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland constituency are set to benefit from sweeping new support for families after Labour’s flagship Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Act officially became law this week (Wednesday 29 April 2026).

The wide-ranging reforms — backed by local MP Luke Myer and opposed by both Conservatives and Reform — will expand free school meals, roll out breakfast clubs, and slash the cost of school uniforms, with some families expected to save up to £1,000 a year.

From September, up to 4,770 children in our constituency locally will also qualify for free school meals under the new expanded offer, while 1,578 children will benefit from new funded breakfast clubs in the first wave alone. Schools included are Chaloner Primary, Handale Primary, Lingdale Primary, Whitecliffe Primary Academy, Skelton Primary, Lockwood Primary, Pennyman Primary Academy and St Bernadette’s Catholic Primary School. The Government has pledged that every primary school will have access to a free breakfast club by the end of the Parliament.

The changes come against a stark backdrop, with 31.3% of children across the Tees Valley currently living in poverty. Ministers say the reforms are part of a wider push to break the link between background and success – with a national Child Poverty Strategy aiming to lift over half a million children out of poverty by tackling costs, boosting incomes, and improving local services.

Alongside the cost-of-living support, the Act also delivers major safeguarding reforms — strengthening protections for vulnerable children, cracking down on unsafe care providers, and ensuring no child falls through the cracks. Schools will also be required to limit branded uniform items, helping ease pressure on family budgets.

Luke Myer MP said:

“This is about putting money back into families’ pockets and giving every child the best possible start in life. Free breakfast clubs, cheaper uniforms, and expanded school meals will make a real difference to thousands of families across our area.”

“In a place like ours, where too many children are growing up in poverty, breaking down barriers to opportunity is essential if we want our region’s economy to succeed. These reforms are a big step towards making sure every child, wherever they grow up, has the chance to get on in life.”

Education Secretary Bridget Phillipson said:  

“This landmark legislation fixes the foundations in our schools, puts money back into parents’ pockets, and provides critical new protections for our most vulnerable children.  

“From lifting the two-child limit to transforming the life chances of children with SEND, this government is already making strides to cut the link between background and success.  

“These new laws are another major milestone in our mission to make sure every child – wherever they grow up and go to school – has the opportunity to succeed.”

Frank Young, Chief Executive of Parentkind said:  

“Millions of parents are struggling with added school costs like expensive school uniform. Rising costs are hitting everyone hard, but parents have school costs they have no option but to pay. The Act will make a difference to these parents by tackling the cost of living and putting limits on expensive uniform items. This will help struggling parents, including many on middle incomes too.”
 
“Free breakfast clubs and cheaper uniforms will help parents and tackle some of the extra costs that make life more difficult for parents. Getting more breakfast clubs going helps parents to hold down a job and get kids to school.”

John McCabe, chief executive of the North East Chamber of Commerce, said:

“Tackling poverty is fundamental to improving educational outcomes, widening access to opportunity and supporting people into good quality work. Every day, I hear from business leaders who need people with the right skills and stability to drive their organisations forward. Reducing child poverty is how we build that pipeline. If we want the North East to compete on the global stage, we must start by ensuring every child can thrive.“

“The abolition of the two-child benefit cap is welcome and will make a tangible difference to families across the North East.“

“We will continue to work with our members to press for meaningful regional and national action to tackle child poverty and its wide-ranging impacts on North East communities and our economy.”

item.title: Industry leaders convened in Parliament for Carbon Capture push
item.dc:creator: luke
item.description: Today (29 April 2026) Luke Myer MP brought together industry leaders in Westminster to drive forward plans for cleaner growth and jobs for Teesside. Luke Myer MP chaired the roundtable in Parliament on Wednesday with leaders from across the rail freight, energy and industrial sectors to discuss the future of non-pipeline transport of CO₂ – […]
item.content:encoded:
Today (29 April 2026) Luke Myer MP brought together industry leaders in Westminster to drive forward plans for cleaner growth and jobs for Teesside.

Luke Myer MP chaired the roundtable in Parliament on Wednesday with leaders from across the rail freight, energy and industrial sectors to discuss the future of non-pipeline transport of CO₂ – a key part of delivering carbon capture, usage and storage (CCUS) in the UK.

The carbon capture industry is rapidly scaling up on Teesside, following the £4bn Northern Endurance Partnership project being granted approval from the Labour Government in 2024.

The meeting today brought together organisations including Navigator Terminals, the Rail Freight Group, trade associations, officials from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero, NEPIC and major industrial leaders. Contributions from industry and government highlighted both the scale of the opportunity and the practical challenges that must be addressed to make non-pipeline transport viable at scale.

The roundtable forms part of Luke’s ongoing work as a voice on steel, energy and industrial strategy, and his engagement with businesses across Teesside and the wider North East.

Luke Myer MP said:

“Teesside has always been at the forefront of Britain’s industry, and carbon capture is a huge opportunity for our future. Bringing together industry and government in Parliament is about turning that potential into reality – creating good jobs, backing our industries, and making sure places like ours lead the clean energy transition.”

item.title: Luke leads talks to tackle antisocial behaviour in Coulby Newham
item.dc:creator: Carolanne Bernard
item.description: 24 April 2026 Local MP Luke Myer has brought together key partners today to discuss crime and antisocial behaviour in Coulby Newham. The meeting brought together local PCC Matt Storey and Cllr David Branson for Coulby Newham, alongside representatives from Cleveland Police, Thirteen Housing and the Middlesbrough Council Neighbourhoods Team. While the primary focus was on recent crime and antisocial behaviour that has […]
item.content:encoded:
24 April 2026

Local MP Luke Myer has brought together key partners today to discuss crime and antisocial behaviour in Coulby Newham.

The meeting brought together local PCC Matt Storey and Cllr David Branson for Coulby Newham, alongside representatives from Cleveland Police, Thirteen Housing and the Middlesbrough Council Neighbourhoods Team.

While the primary focus was on recent crime and antisocial behaviour that has taken place in Coulby Newham, the discussion also covered trends across south Middlesbrough more widely, with partners sharing intelligence and coordinating their response across neighbouring communities – particularly around the Parkway Centre and nearby estates. 

It comes as Cleveland Police continues to expand neighbourhood policing, with 48 additional officers and PCSOs now deployed across the force area as part of the Government’s drive to get more officers back on the beat. Strategies used were discussed and assessed: including increased visible patrols, new rapid deployment teams and strengthened engagement with businesses and residents in the local area.

Police and partners set out the work already underway to tackle antisocial behaviour, including targeted patrols in hotspot areas, regular visits to local businesses, and the use of CCTV to help identify those responsible. A permanent CCTV camera is planned for Dalby Way, alongside further temporary cameras to respond to issues as they arise.

Wider challenges were also discussed, including a rise in shop theft, ongoing problems with off road bikes, and concerns about antisocial behaviour linked to some privately rented properties where it can be harder to take action.

MP for Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland, Luke Myer said:

“Putting 48 new neighbourhood officers on the beat in Cleveland Police is a really positive step and something local people have been calling for, but we know there is more to do. We only get on top of antisocial behaviour when everyone works as one team. Local police, the council, housing associations and local communities all have a role to play, and meetings like this are how we make that happen.”

Councillor for Coulby Newham, David Branson said:

“Residents have raised concerns about hotspots like Longhurst and Dalby Way, as well as wider issues like off-road bikes and problems linked to some privately rented properties. It is important that partners keep working together and have the powers needed to deal with serious antisocial behaviour.”

Further meetings with partners and local businesses are planned as part of ongoing work to drive down crime and improve safety across Coulby Newham and south Middlesbrough.

item.title: Sea change for Teesside clean power jobs
item.dc:creator: luke
item.description: Offshore wind in the North East has been given a major boost today (27 April 2026), as new rules have been passed in Parliament to unlock investment and speed up clean energy projects. The changes overhaul how environmental protections are applied at sea. Instead of developers being limited to very narrow, site-by-site fixes, they will […]
item.content:encoded:
Offshore wind in the North East has been given a major boost today (27 April 2026), as new rules have been passed in Parliament to unlock investment and speed up clean energy projects.

The changes overhaul how environmental protections are applied at sea. Instead of developers being limited to very narrow, site-by-site fixes, they will now be able to deliver general environmental improvements across the wider marine area. That means projects can go ahead faster, while still protecting nature.

Luke Myer, MP for Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland, served on the committee which passed the reforms and said they could help drive jobs and growth across Teesside.

Luke Myer MP said:

“This is about backing British energy, creating jobs here at home and bringing bills down in the long run by boosting our own supply. Teesside can and should lead the way.”

“People on Teesside want good jobs, lower bills and a secure future. With common sense regulations in place, offshore wind can help deliver all three. This is about making sure Britain can become a clean energy superpower, and Teesside can lead the way.”

Teesside is already a clean energy powerhouse, with around 9,000 jobs in the sector across the region. By speeding up consent approvals, the region can bring in even more investment and create more skilled jobs in turbine manufacture and related industries.

item.title: No 10 reception for Guisborough Town after historic promotion
item.dc:creator: luke
item.description: 24 April – Article from The Northern Echo A football club director has been honoured at Downing Street following the club’s first promotion in more than 40 years. Chris Wood, director of Guisborough Town FC, was invited to a St George’s Day reception at 10 Downing Street by Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland MP Luke […]
item.content:encoded:
24 April – Article from The Northern Echo

A football club director has been honoured at Downing Street following the club’s first promotion in more than 40 years.

Chris Wood, director of Guisborough Town FC, was invited to a St George’s Day reception at 10 Downing Street by Middlesbrough South and East Cleveland MP Luke Myer.

The visit came in recognition of Guisborough Town’s historic rise to the Northern Premier League – their first promotion in over four decades.

Mr Wood said:

“It was an incredible experience and an honour to represent Guisborough Town Football Club following the promotion, and everyone’s continued commitment to have our Club at the heart of the community.

“At the event, we heard from Olympians Laura and Jason Kenny and the Prime Minister about community champions from across the country, who showcase English values.

“Not forgetting some of the best of English food around the reception.”

“Thanks to Luke Myer MP for the invitation and his continued support of Guisborough Town Football Club.”

Mr Wood has played a key role behind the scenes at Guisborough Town for more than 10 years, having been involved with the club since childhood.

Mr Myer said:

“After more than 40 years, to see the town come together to cheer on the club and see them finally earn promotion is a brilliant milestone.

“It is down to all the fans, the players, and everyone at the club who have put in the hard work behind the scenes, week in, week out.

“I was proud to bring Chris to Downing Street to make sure that effort was recognised.”

The Downing Street reception celebrated community champions from across England, with guests hearing from Olympic champions and national figures.

Speakers highlighted the importance of grassroots leadership in building stronger, more vibrant communities.

Guisborough Town’s promotion has been widely celebrated by supporters and the community, marking a major milestone in the club’s long history.

The achievement has reignited enthusiasm both on and off the pitch as the club prepares for its next chapter in a higher league.

Supporters hope the momentum will continue to build as Guisborough Town enters this new era of competition.

Article from The Northern Echo – read the full article here

item.title: Luke keeps up pressure on fleecehold scandal
item.dc:creator: luke
item.description: Luke Myer MP has taken the fight on “fleecehold” straight to the top — confronting the Housing Minister in a face-to-face meeting today (22 April 2026) and demanding action for homeowners hit with unfair charges. Residents across East Cleveland and South Middlesbrough have said they feel “trapped” – paying rising estate fees with little transparency, […]
item.content:encoded:
Luke Myer MP has taken the fight on “fleecehold” straight to the top — confronting the Housing Minister in a face-to-face meeting today (22 April 2026) and demanding action for homeowners hit with unfair charges.

Residents across East Cleveland and South Middlesbrough have said they feel “trapped” – paying rising estate fees with little transparency, no proper say, and no clear route for their streets to be adopted. Luke made clear that while consultations are ongoing, families want real change, not more delay.

This latest push is the culmination of months of pressure. Luke first raised fleecehold in Parliament last April, before hosting two resident roundtables over the summer in South Middlesbrough and Guisborough. He then took residents’ experiences straight to Government in a formal submission – warning ministers they needed a dedicated crackdown on fleecehold, not just leasehold reform. Since then, he’s kept up the pressure – grilling ministers in the Commons in January, listening to residents in Nunthorpe at a dedicated meeting on unadopted estates, meeting developers in Stainton, and tabling parliamentary questions to keep the issue firmly on the agenda.

Ministers have begun to move, with proposals on the table to curb the worst legal powers used against homeowners, force greater transparency over charges, and give residents new rights to challenge rogue management companies. But Luke made clear today: it’s not enough yet. He committed to keep pushing until residents get a fair deal.

item.title: Luke meets new police officers in Guisborough
item.dc:creator: luke
item.description: 17 April 2026 Residents in Guisborough today spoke with some of Cleveland Police’s new PCSOs and officers at their community safety drop-in at Sunnyfield House. Dozens of extra neighbourhood officers and PCSOs have been rolled out across Cleveland – putting visible policing back on the streets. A total of 48 new neighbourhood police and PCSOs […]
item.content:encoded:
17 April 2026

Residents in Guisborough today spoke with some of Cleveland Police’s new PCSOs and officers at their community safety drop-in at Sunnyfield House.

Dozens of extra neighbourhood officers and PCSOs have been rolled out across Cleveland – putting visible policing back on the streets. A total of 48 new neighbourhood police and PCSOs been deployed locally, part of a wider drive to crack down on the crimes people see every day.

Luke Myer MP has consistently pushed for tougher action on crime and more resources for community policing. Earlier this week, he again raised the issue of the police funding formula with the Policing Minister, saying:

“We have a small population but high deprivation, and the police funding formula does not take account of the severity of crime in our region.”

In response, the minister acknowledged “the current police funding formula is not fit for purpose” and it “needs reform”. She committed to “review the formula to bring it up to date and make it fit for purpose”.

Earlier this week, Luke also voted for the Crime & Policing Bill to progress to its next stage – which includes sweeping new powers to tackle crime, including scrapping the £200 “low-level theft” rule, introducing tough new Respect Orders to deal with persistent antisocial behaviour, and new protections for shopworkers facing abuse.

Luke Myer MP said:

“People have had enough of feeling like nothing gets done. They want to see police in their communities, dealing with the problems right in front of them.

“This is about getting back to basics: visible policing, proper action, and making sure people feel safe where they live.”

item.title: Energy bills slashed for 10,000 manufacturers
item.dc:creator: luke
item.description: Today (16 April 2026), the Labour Government announced a major cut to industrial electricity bills, slashing costs by up to 25% for more than 10,000 manufacturers as part of its Plan for Change. The move expands the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme, bringing in thousands more firms across sectors like automotive, aerospace, chemicals and the wider […]
item.content:encoded:
Today (16 April 2026), the Labour Government announced a major cut to industrial electricity bills, slashing costs by up to 25% for more than 10,000 manufacturers as part of its Plan for Change.

The move expands the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme, bringing in thousands more firms across sectors like automotive, aerospace, chemicals and the wider steel supply chain. For businesses that have been battered by sky-high energy costs, it’s a serious intervention – cutting bills, backing jobs, and giving British industry a better shot at competing globally.

Luke Myer MP has raised the issue repeatedly in Parliament – hosting roundtables with rural manufacturers, pushing ministers on grid connections, and pressing the Government to go further to close the energy price gap with international competitors. As Chair of the APPG on Steel and Metals-Related Industries, he has been a consistent voice for Teesside’s energy-intensive industries, including British Steel and smaller SMEs in the region.

While the announcement is a significant step forward and has been welcomed by the steel industry, industry voices have made clear that steelmakers remain exposed to soaring wholesale electricity prices. UK producers are paying significantly more than competitors in France and Germany – putting pressure on jobs and investment.

That’s why Luke is continuing to press for further action to bring down wholesale prices for industry and residents. He is committed to push ministers to tackle the underlying cost of electricity and ensure Britain’s steel industry can compete on a level playing field. The Government’s wider industrial strategy is starting to turn the tide after years of decline, but for places like Teesside, the job isn’t finished yet.

Luke Myer MP said:

“This is a big step in the right direction. Cutting energy bills for thousands of manufacturers will help protect jobs, bring costs down, and back British industry to compete and win.

“But for communities like ours, built on steel and heavy industry, we have to go further. Our steelmakers are still facing energy costs far higher than their competitors overseas.

“I’ll keep pushing the Government to close that gap, back our steelworkers, and make sure Teesside is at the heart of Britain’s industrial future.”

Frank Aaskov, Energy Policy Director, UK Steel, said: 

“The BICS will bring welcome relief for parts of the steel supply chain and manufacturers not currently covered by existing schemes and materially lower their energy bills.

But it will not lower electricity prices for steel producers themselves, who remain exposed to exceptionally high wholesale power costs. That problem has intensified sharply in recent months. As a result of the Middle East war, UK steelmakers are now paying nearly 80% more for electricity than competitors in France and Germany, up from around 25% previously. This is happening despite the support already in place and reflects the UK’s continued exposure to gas‑driven electricity prices.

“To make the Steel Strategy a success and deliver the Government’s industrial and decarbonisation ambitions, additional measures are now essential. That means targeted action to bring wholesale electricity prices into line with our European competitors that gives industry the confidence to invest.”

item.title: Luke backs jobs & apprenticeships in Parliament
item.dc:creator: luke
item.description: 15 April 2026 Hundreds of skilled Teesside jobs are moving closer after talks in Parliament led by local MP Luke Myer. Working with the Carbon Capture & Storage Association, Luke brought together industry leaders, ministers and apprentices for discussions in Parliament on carbon capture – one of the biggest new industries coming to Teesside following […]
item.content:encoded:
15 April 2026

Hundreds of skilled Teesside jobs are moving closer after talks in Parliament led by local MP Luke Myer.

Working with the Carbon Capture & Storage Association, Luke brought together industry leaders, ministers and apprentices for discussions in Parliament on carbon capture – one of the biggest new industries coming to Teesside following the Labour Government’s decision to move ahead with £4bn investment in the region. At the heart of the meeting were two young apprentices, Sophie and Sarah, who started working on the project with BP in September. They are part of the next generation set to benefit from billions of pounds of investment for Teesside as the region builds the UK’s first CO₂ transport and storage network.

Energy Minister Michael Shanks joined Luke at the event, meeting apprentices and hearing directly from businesses about the opportunities ahead. In a follow-up meeting with Minister Shanks this afternoon, Luke pressed hard on the barriers facing local firms including grid capacity, slow connections and high energy costs. The Minister agreed to take the issue away, and promised that further action on industrial energy costs would be imminent.

Luke Myer MP also met with Secretary of State for Business & Trade Peter Kyle today, where they discussed energy, defence manufacturing, and the future of British steel. In all discussions, Luke was clear that Teesside stands ready to deliver new jobs and industries.

item.title: £2bn Teesside fuel project fast-tracked
item.dc:creator: luke
item.description: 12 April 2026 A major £2 billion Teesside fuel project backed by Luke Myer MP has been given a significant boost after the Labour Government moved to speed up approvals – bringing thousands of jobs a step closer. The government has confirmed that the Environment Agency will act as a single “lead regulator” for Lighthouse […]
item.content:encoded:
12 April 2026

A major £2 billion Teesside fuel project backed by Luke Myer MP has been given a significant boost after the Labour Government moved to speed up approvals – bringing thousands of jobs a step closer.

The government has confirmed that the Environment Agency will act as a single “lead regulator” for Lighthouse Green Fuels, cutting red tape and accelerating decisions so construction can move forward faster. The move is part of wider action to get major clean energy projects built more quickly, protecting families from rising bills while backing British industry.

The Lighthouse Green Fuels project by Alfanar, is set to transform waste and agricultural residues into sustainable aviation fuel from their plant on Teesside. It is expected to create around 2,000 construction jobs in Teesside and thousands more across the UK supply chain, establishing the region as the heartland for a new industry.

The project is set to become Europe’s largest sustainable aviation fuel plant — producing enough fuel for tens of thousands of flights each year — and marking the first major refinery development in the UK for decades. With global energy markets under pressure, ministers say speeding up projects like Lighthouse Green Fuels will strengthen Britain’s energy security, create jobs, and help bring down bills in the long run — with Teesside at the heart of that effort.

Today’s announcement follows years of work from Luke Myer MP to push the project forward with government. Since being elected, he has met repeatedly with developers, visited the site, and raised the importance of sustainable aviation fuel in Parliament. He recently met the team in Parliament last month.

Luke Myer MP said:

“This is a big step forward for Teesside. This is exactly the kind of project our area needs: skilled jobs, real investment, and a proper industrial future.

“We’ve now got government moving to cut delays and back this properly. For too long, places like ours have had the ideas and the talent but been held back by slow decisions. We’ve got the sites, the skills and the know-how on Teesside to lead in industry again, and this project shows what’s possible when we back British industry and get on with it.”

Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Emma Reynolds said:

“We are taking back control of our energy supply to bring stability for families and create skilled jobs for local people, without compromising on environmental protections.

“Global shocks from conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East show that relying on a volatile global fossil fuel market is simply not sustainable for Britain. These measures are a win-win for energy security, nature and for keeping bills down in the long run.”

Noaman Al Adhami, UK Country Head for Alfanar Projects, said:

“We welcome the opportunity to support Defra’s Lead Environmental Regulator pilot, which represents an important step forward in streamlining engagement with statutory bodies and accelerating the delivery of major infrastructure projects.

“By enabling earlier, more coordinated regulatory input, this initiative will help unlock investment and support the timely progression of projects like Lighthouse Green Fuels as we advance towards construction.

“This pilot also plays a vital role in supporting the government’s wider ambition to accelerate infrastructure delivery and strengthen its industrial strategy. We are proud that LGF can contribute to this effort while helping to establish a new low-carbon industry in the UK.”
